Stretched PTFE nanofiber nonwovens for superior air permeability and dust holding capacity in PM filtration Ji Hyun Lee a,b, Yoonjin Kim a, Hye Sun Hwang c, Gyudong Lee a , Song Jun Doh a, Chulsang Youn d, Honghee Lee e

, Seon-Jin Choi b,*, Ki Ro Yoon a,f,** a Textile Innovation R&D Department, Korea Institute of Industrial Technology, 143, Hanggaulro, Sangnok-gu, Ansan, Gyeonggi-do 15588, Republic of Koreab Division of Materials Science and Engineering, Hanyang University, 222, Wangsimni-ro, Seongdong-gu, Seoul 04763, Republic of Koreac Safety Convergence Technology R&D department, Korea Institute of Industrial Technology, 143, Hanggaulro, Sangnok-gu, Ansan, Gyeonggi-do 15588, Republic of Koread Reliability Research Center, FITI Testing and Research Institute, 79, Magok-Jungang 8-ro 3-gil, Seoul 07791, Republic of Koreae Material Digital Twin Lab, Trinity Engineering, #904 Hyundai41Tower, 293 Mokdongdong-ro, Yangchun-gu, Seoul 07997, Republic of Koreaf School of Integrative Engineering, Chung-Ang University, Seoul 06974, Republic of Korea

A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords:Emulsification electrospinningStretchingPTFE nanofiberAir filtration mediaHigh dust holding capacityAerosol deposition simulation

citation.title: Stretched PTFE nanofiber nonwovens for superior air permeability and dust holding capacity in PM filtration

content: A B S T R A C T In industrial environments, the massive emission of particulate matter (PM) and dusts, along with high tem­peratures, humidity, and exposure to toxic gases and chemicals, necessitate the development of industrial filtration media with high durability and enhanced dust holding capacity. To address these challenges, we propose a novel approach to extend the operational lifespan of high-performance filtration media by employing stretched polytetrafluoroethylene (sPTFE) nanofiber (NF) nonwovens. Utilizing an emulsification electrospinning process followed by an advanced stretching technique, sPTFE NFs were fabricated and tailored to optimize key microstructural properties, such as thickness, fiber diameter, and pore size. Notably, stretching the PTFE NF by 2and 3-fold resulted in significant fiber thinning, effectively increasing internal pore size and substantially enhancing air permeability. The 3-fold stretched sPTFE (3-sPTFE) NF not only retained its nanofibrous air filtration properties but also improved its crystalline structural stability, achieving a high filtration efficiency (>92%) via a depth filtration mechanism, even with a reduced media thickness (28.96 μm), while exhibiting a lower pressure drop of 60.8 Pa.

citation.title: Stretched PTFE nanofiber nonwovens for superior air permeability and dust holding capacity in PM filtration

content: efficiency (>92%) via a depth filtration mechanism, even with a reduced media thickness (28.96 μm), while exhibiting a lower pressure drop of 60.8 Pa. More importantly, the 3-sPTFE filter demonstrated an extended lifespan of 16,281 s and an a superior dust holding capacity of 107.7 g m-2, outperforming both less-stretched PTFE NFs and conventional expanded PTFE (ePTFE) membranes, which rely primarily on surface filtration. These results establish sPTFE NFs as a highly promising solution for industrial PM filtration, offering enhanced operational efficiency, prolonged usability, and reliable performance in harsh emission environments.

citation.title: Stretched PTFE nanofiber nonwovens for superior air permeability and dust holding capacity in PM filtration

content: 1. Introduction Airborne particulate matter (PM) emissions pose serious environ­mental threats and significant health risks, as prolonged exposure has been linked to respiratory diseases, cardiovascular disorders, and even premature mortality [1–4]. Particularly, industrial environments contribute to large-scale emissions of PM and dusts, necessitating the use of high-capacity, long-lasting filtration system. Common industrial filtration technologies include bag filter, high-efficiency particulate air (HEPA) filters, electrostatic precipitators (ESPs), and cartridge filters, each designed to capture fine particles from emission sources. However, these systems often suffer from frequent clogging, high pressure drops, leading to high energy consumption, and increased maintenance costs via frequent replacement cycles, ultimately leading to significant oper­ational and economic burdens [5–8]. Furthermore, many conventional filters are prone to degradation when exposed to harsh industrial con­ditions, such as high temperatures, humidity and chemically aggressive environments (e.g., corrosive gases and oxidative agents). These factors collectively limit their long-term effectiveness and reliability in demanding industrial applications [9–12]. To address these challenges,

citation.title: Stretched PTFE nanofiber nonwovens for superior air permeability and dust holding capacity in PM filtration

citation.title: Stretched PTFE nanofiber nonwovens for superior air permeability and dust holding capacity in PM filtration

content: Available online 2 June 2025 0376-7388/ 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC license ( http://creativecommons.org/licenses/bync/4.0/ ). there is an urgent need for a next-generation industrial filtration media that combines thermal and chemical stability, enhanced dust holding capacity, low pressure drop, and an extended operational lifetime. Olefin-based polymers such as polyethylene (PE), polypropylene (PP), and polyethylene terephthalate (PET) are commonly employed in filtration systems due to their affordability and wide availability [13–15]. However, these polymers have relatively low melting points (less than 250 ◦C) and limited chemical resistance, resulting in reduced durability when exposed to the harsh operating conditions often encountered in industrial sites. In contrast, polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) has emerged as a promising alternative for industrial applica­tions, particularly in chemically aggressive and high-temperature envi­ronments, due to its exceptional chemical resistance, thermal stability, and non-reactive surface properties. Recently, expanded PTFE (ePTFE) membranes, produced by stretching PTFE preformed films, have been used in air filtration systems, especially in cleanroom and indoor air conditioning applications where dust emissions are relatively low [16].

citation.title: Stretched PTFE nanofiber nonwovens for superior air permeability and dust holding capacity in PM filtration

content: ve been used in air filtration systems, especially in cleanroom and indoor air conditioning applications where dust emissions are relatively low [16]. The stretching process induces fibrillation, forming a physical sieving media with interconnected pores between the fibrils that allows air permeability. However, the dense, sub-100 nm pores of ePTFE mem­brane generate a high pressure drop and primarily rely on surface filtration, leading to rapid clogging as dust accumulates on the surface and diminishing functionality over time. This limitation hinders the broader application of ePTFE membranes in industrial sites with high PM emissions. Nonwoven-based filtration media, produced using traditional spinning processes such as melt spinning (spun-bond or melt-blown), wet spinning, and electrospinning, enable particle capture throughout the filter body, following a depth filtration mechanism that ensures sufficient filtration performance and extends operational life­time. However, due to its high melting point (327 ◦C) and insolubility in most solvents, PTFE is unsuitable for these conventional spinning methods, which presents a challenge that must be addressed. Recently, Lee et al., reported a breakthrough in fabricating PTFE nanofiber (NF) networks using an emulsification electrospinning tech­nique, offering high performance and durability in filtration applica­tions [17].

citation.title: Stretched PTFE nanofiber nonwovens for superior air permeability and dust holding capacity in PM filtration

content: nanofiber (NF) networks using an emulsification electrospinning tech­nique, offering high performance and durability in filtration applica­tions [17]. In this method, PTFE particles are dispersed in a sacrificial polymer matrix, such as polyethylene oxide (PEO), allowing the for­mation of PEO/PTFE NF networks. A subsequent thermal treatment removes the sacrificial polymer, leaving behind a robust nanofibrous PTFE structure that addresses the aforementioned limitations of existing filtration media. Nevertheless, electrospun NF networks often experi­ence high pressure drops due to the high density of nanoscale fibers [18]. Although reducing thickness by decreasing spinning time may lower pressure drop, it also limits the available space for dust capture, thereby impacting filtration efficiency. Achieving a balance between high filtration performance, low pressure drop, and sufficient dust-holding capacity while ensuring scalability remains crucial for the practical application of PTFE NF nonwovens in industrial filtration. Herein, we present a breakthrough approach for the fabrication of a high-efficiency stretched PTFE (sPTFE) NF filter, utilizing a novel stretching technique that precisely controls microstructural properties, thereby significantly reducing pressure drop across the filtration media.

citation.title: Stretched PTFE nanofiber nonwovens for superior air permeability and dust holding capacity in PM filtration

content: vel stretching technique that precisely controls microstructural properties, thereby significantly reducing pressure drop across the filtration media. The PTFE NF was stretched up to 3-fold (3-sPTFE NF) along both the machine (MD) and transverse directions (TD), inducing critical micro­structural modifications, such as a reduction in overall filter thickness, decreased fiber diameter, and increased internal pore size. These structural transformations create a highly porous nanofibrous network that effectively maintains high PM filtration efficiency while substan­tially lowering airflow resistance. Remarkably, despite the extensive stretching process, the inherent crystalline structure of nanofibrous PTFE remains intact and is even further stabilized, ensuring effective depth filtration across a large surface area, leading to a significantly extended operational lifespan and dust holding capacity (16,821 s and 107.7 g m-2 for 3-sPTFE NF, respectively) in continuous particulate filtration tests, over three times longer and larger than the conventional ePTFE membrane filter (5,440 s and 29.8 g m-2, respectively). By synergizing the exceptional chemical and thermal stability of PTFE with engineered nanofibrous microstructures, the sPTFE NF represents a highly promising solution for next-generation industrial air filtration, particularly in harsh environments requiring high efficiency, low pres­sure drop, and extended filter longevity. 2. Experimental section 2.1.

citation.title: Stretched PTFE nanofiber nonwovens for superior air permeability and dust holding capacity in PM filtration

content: tration, particularly in harsh environments requiring high efficiency, low pres­sure drop, and extended filter longevity. 2. Experimental section 2.1. Synthesis of PTFE NF The emulsification electrospinning solution was prepared by dis­solving 0.4 g of PEO (MW = 600,000 g mol-1, Sigma-Aldrich) and 2.5 g N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, Sigma-Aldrich) in 5 g of deionized (DI) water with vigorously stirring at 70 ◦C for 5 h, followed by adding 5.5 g of Teflon dispersion (60 wt.%, NARA Cell-Tech) after cooling prepared solution. The solution containing PEO and PTFE particles was trans­ferred into a 12 mL syringe, and a 21-gauge needle was capped on the syringe tip. A feeding rate of 1.0 mL h-1 and a high voltage of 10 kV were applied between the needle and the stainless-steel wrapped collector, which were separated by a distance of 15 cm. Meanwhile, the as-spun PEO/PTFE NF was thermally treated in the air at 400 ◦C for 10 min with ramping rates of 10 ◦C min-1. 2.2. Preparation of 2-sPTFE and 3-sPTFE NFs The 2-sPTFE and 3-sPTFE NFs were fabricated using the stretching machine (KARO 5.0, Brückner Maschinenbau, Siegsdorf, Germany), which has controllable parameters such as heating temperature/time, stretching ratio/speed, and annealing temperature/time. Firstly, heattreated PTFE NF was placed in a rectangular sample jig and secured with clips in the stretching machine.

citation.title: Stretched PTFE nanofiber nonwovens for superior air permeability and dust holding capacity in PM filtration

content: and annealing temperature/time. Firstly, heattreated PTFE NF was placed in a rectangular sample jig and secured with clips in the stretching machine. The jig with the fixed sample was transferred to the stretching chamber, where the sample was heated to 320 ◦C for 80 s via a thermal diffuser. The sample was then stretched at a speed of 50 mm s-1 with various ratios (2or 3-fold). The jig was sub­sequently transferred to the annealing chamber, where the sample was heat treated at 340 ◦C for 300 s to obtain 2-sPTFE and 3-sPTFE NFs. 2.3. Material characterization The microstructures of the prepared samples were examined using field-emission SEM (FE-SEM, SU8010, Hitachi Co., Tokyo, Japan), and the composition were investigated through FT-IR spectroscopy (Spec­trum Two, PerkinElmer Inc., MA, USA). TGA (Q500, TA Instruments, DE, USA) was performed at a heating rate of 20 ◦C min-1 under a ni­trogen atmosphere in the temperature range from 40 to 800 ◦C. The strain-stress curves were assessed using a universal testing system (3300, INSTRON, MA, USA). Samples measuring 5 * 50 mm2 were cut, and the tensile test was conducted at a speed of 10 mm min-1. A capillary flow porometer (CFP-1500AEX, Porous Materials Inc., NY, USA) was used to observe the pore distribution and mean pore diameter of nanofibrous PTFE. The porosities of the prepared samples were measured using a mercury porosimetry (AutoPore IV, Micromeritics Instrument Co., GA, USA).

citation.title: Stretched PTFE nanofiber nonwovens for superior air permeability and dust holding capacity in PM filtration

content: nofibrous PTFE. The porosities of the prepared samples were measured using a mercury porosimetry (AutoPore IV, Micromeritics Instrument Co., GA, USA). DSC (Q100, TA Instruments, DE, USA) was performed at a heating rate of 10 ◦C min-1 under a nitrogen atmosphere in the temperature range from 30 to 400 ◦C. The degree of crystallinity (Xc) was determined by measuring the melting enthalpy (ΔHm) calcu­lated using the following equation: Xc (%) = ΔHm /ΔH0m(1) where ΔH0m is the 100% crystalline PTFE (80 J g-1). The crystalline structure of nanofibrous PTFE were characterized using XRD (D/ Max–2500/PC, Rigaku Co., Tokyo, Japan). The average crystal size (L) of was calculated using the Scherrer equation: L = K λ/β cos θ(2)

content: J.H. Lee et al. Journal of Membrane Science 733 (2025) 124292 where K is the shape factor of 0.89, λ is the X-ray wavelength of Cu Kα radiation source (1.5418 Å), β is the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the diffraction peak in radians, and θ is the Bragg angle. The. 2.4. Filtration performance test The comparative ePTFE sample used in this study was a E11-grade (>95.0% in filtration efficiency) membrane supplied by Micro-One Co., Ltd. (Korea). The air permeability test was confirmed using an air permeability tester (FX 3300, TEXTEST, Schwerzenbach, Switzerland) at a test pressure of 125 Pa. The samples were clamped over the air inlet using test heads, and the air was sucked through the pump. The preset test pressure was automatically maintained, and the air permeability of the filters was calculated in cm3 cm-2 s-1. The filtration efficiency and pressure drop were measured using an automated air filtration tester (TSI 8130, TSI Inc., MN, USA). The flow rate was set to 32 L min-1, and the sodium chloride (NaCl) aerosol particles used had a mean diameter of approximately 260 nm, corresponding to PM0.3. The air filtration media were placed on a jig, when the NaCl aerosol passed through the filtration media, the upstream and downstream aerosol concentrations were measured simultaneously through a laser and optics photometer system.

citation.title: Stretched PTFE nanofiber nonwovens for superior air permeability and dust holding capacity in PM filtration

content: gh the filtration media, the upstream and downstream aerosol concentrations were measured simultaneously through a laser and optics photometer system. The filtration efficiency (η) was calculated using the following equation: η = Cup /Cdown(3) where Cup and Cdown are NaCl aerosol concentrations of upstream and downstream, respectively. The quality factor (Qf) value was obtained using the following equation: Qf = -In(1 -η) / ΔP(4) where ΔP is the pressure drop across the filtration samples. Each sample was measured 5 times, and the average value was calculated. The longterm filtration performance was evaluated during aerosol loading at a continuous airflow (32 L min-1) until reaching the maximum pressure drop of 1,471 Pa (150 mmH2O). The dust holding capacity represents the amount of aerosol captured within the test filter and is quantified for each sample as the difference between its initial mass and its mass after the aerosol loading test at a maximum pressure drop of 1,471 Pa. 2.5. Boundary condition of GeoDict-FilterDict for aerosol deposition simulation Simulations were performed using GeoDict (Math2Market, Fraunhofer Institute, Kaiserslautern, Germany), a commercially avail­able simulation software for microstructure analysis. Filtration simula­tions were specifically performed utilizing the FilterDict module within GeoDict.

citation.title: Stretched PTFE nanofiber nonwovens for superior air permeability and dust holding capacity in PM filtration

content: e simulation software for microstructure analysis. Filtration simula­tions were specifically performed utilizing the FilterDict module within GeoDict. To assess the filtration performance of the SEM image-based 3D model, boundary conditions were meticulously set to closely replicate the experimental conditions. The implementation of FilterDict simula­tions necessitates the precise input of corresponding boundary condi­tions (A detailed information is described in Table S1). The experimental fluid employed was a 2 wt.% NaCl solution, with a flow rate of 32 L min-1. These parameters were directly translated into the simulation settings. The filter media used in the experiments had a surface area of 100 cm2. Although the modeled surface area was approximately 1.215 μm2, the simulation domain was adjusted to reflect the actual filter area of 100 cm2 used in the experiments. While the actual experimental duration was approximately 4 h, simulations aimed at evaluating initial differential pressure and filter efficiency do not require such extended durations. Therefore, a simulation time equiva­lent to one-tenth of the actual experimental time was adopted. For comparative assessments involving complete filters, replicating the full experimental duration would be necessary. However, as this study fo­cuses on filter media rather than complete filters, the simulation parameters were adjusted as outlined in Table S1.

citation.title: Stretched PTFE nanofiber nonwovens for superior air permeability and dust holding capacity in PM filtration

content: essary. However, as this study fo­cuses on filter media rather than complete filters, the simulation parameters were adjusted as outlined in Table S1. The particle diameter distribution in the experiments ranged from 50 to 300 nm, adhering to the test standards. However, the simulation utilized fiber-scale models with diameters in the nanometer range and applied slip boundary con­ditions appropriate for such geometries. A slip length equivalent to 100% of the fiber diameter was applied to account for potential slip effects observed at the nanoscale. Two models (PTFE NF and 3-sPTFE NF) were analyzed with mean fiber diameters of 1 μm and 500 nm, respectively. Given that these dimensions are characteristic of NFs, the use of a slip length of this magnitude is physically justifiable based on previous studies [19,20]. 3. Results and Discussion 3.1. Preparation of PTFE and sPTFE NFs The fabrication of sPTFE NFs involved emulsification electro­spinning, thermal treatment, and physical stretching steps, as sche­matically illustrated in Fig. 1. Since PTFE is insoluble in most polar solvents, pure PTFE NF were produced by emulsifying PTFE particles in a PEO-dissolved DMF solution, enhancing electrospinnability, followed by electrospinning the emulsion to form PTFE particles-embedded PEO (PEO/PTFE) NFs. The thermal decomposition of the PEO component was then carried out at 400 ◦C, as demonstrated in our previous studies [17,21].

citation.title: Stretched PTFE nanofiber nonwovens for superior air permeability and dust holding capacity in PM filtration

content: ed PEO (PEO/PTFE) NFs. The thermal decomposition of the PEO component was then carried out at 400 ◦C, as demonstrated in our previous studies [17,21]. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) showed that PEO de­composes below 400 ◦C, while PTFE remains table up to approximately 500 ◦C (Fig. S1). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) transmittance spectra further verified the complete removal of PEO, as indicated by the disappearance of aliphatic (-CH2) and ether (C–O) peaks associated with PEO, leaving only PTFE-related peaks (-CF2 and –CF3) after heat treat­ment (Fig. S2) [21]. Additionally, energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) elemental mapping of the PEO/PTFE NFs revealed the presence of carbon, oxygen, and fluorine (43.97%, 7.22%, and 48.80%, respec­tively), whereas only carbon and fluorine (36.41% and 63.59%, respectively) were detected in the PTFE NF after annealing (Fig. S3). These results confirm the complete decomposition of PEO at 400 ◦C, successfully yielding a pure PTFE NF nonwovens. Subsequently, a biaxial stretching process was performed to further increase pore size and porosity of as-synthesized PTFE NF. The prepared PTFE NF sheet, cut to dimensions of 90 * 90 mm2, was secured using five clips along each edge of a square-type jig within the stretching machine (KARO 5.0, Brückner Maschinenbau). The detailed experi­mental setup and stretching procedure are described in Fig. S4.

citation.title: Stretched PTFE nanofiber nonwovens for superior air permeability and dust holding capacity in PM filtration

content: g within the stretching machine (KARO 5.0, Brückner Maschinenbau). The detailed experi­mental setup and stretching procedure are described in Fig. S4. To effectively facilitate the mobility of the polymer chains and promote uniform deformation of the nanofibrous matrix [22], the PTFE NF was heated to 320 ◦C for 80 s using a thermal diffuser within the stretching chamber. The PTFE NF was then stretched at 50 mm s-1 to physically attainable stretching ratios of 2and 3-fold. Attempts at 4-fold stretching resulted in excessive thinning and mechanical failure (tearing) of the NF mat, thus exceeding the practical limits of processability. Following the stretching step, the sPTFE NF underwent annealing at 340 ◦C for 5 min in an annealing chamber. This annealing process was essential to pre­vent thermal shrinkage by relaxing internal stress and facilitating the reorganization of PTFE polymer crystal domains, which plays a key role in preserving the mechanical stability of the fibrous structure. The ob­tained 2and 3-fold stretched PTFE NFs were designated as 2-sPTFE NF and 3-sPTFE NF, respectively. 3.2. Microstructural evolution of PTFE and sPTFE NFs Fig. S5 show scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of the asspun PEO/PTFE NFs at low and high magnifications, revealing that PTFE particles with an average size of 220 nm are uniformly embedded within the fibrous PEO matrix.

citation.title: Stretched PTFE nanofiber nonwovens for superior air permeability and dust holding capacity in PM filtration

content: NFs at low and high magnifications, revealing that PTFE particles with an average size of 220 nm are uniformly embedded within the fibrous PEO matrix. Following thermal treatment, the PEO matrix was thermally decomposed, and the remaining PTFE particles fused together while retaining the fibrous structure (Fig. 2A). The pure

citation.title: Stretched PTFE nanofiber nonwovens for superior air permeability and dust holding capacity in PM filtration

content: PTFE NF sheet was placed in the stretching apparatus, as displayed in the inset of Fig. 2A. Fig. 2B and C presents SEM images and corre­sponding photographic images (insets) of 2-sPTFE and 3-sPTFE NFs, which exhibit areal expansions of 400% and 900%, respectively, relative to the original PTFE NF. The SEM images clearly demonstrate that the overall fibrous network is preserved even after stretching. The mean fiber diameter of PTFE NF was measured to be 1.24 μm (Fig. 2D), which decreased to 0.72 and 0.41 μm as the stretching ratios increased to 2and 3-fold, respectively (Fig. 2E and F). This means that the stretching process originates from the thinning of PTFE components within the fibers, driven by the reorganization of polymer domains under applied tensile strain [23]. The original thickness of PTFE NF sheet was approximately 183.10 μm (Fig. 2G), but it significantly decreased to 95.87 and 28.96 μm after 2and 3-fold stretching, respectively (Fig. 2H and I). Furthermore, the unstretched PTFE NF exhibited a basis weight (37.52 g m-2), while the 2-sPTFE and 3-sPTFE NFs showed basis weights of 16.43 and 5.22 g m-2, corresponding to reductions of 56.2% and 86.1%, respectively. These findings demonstrate that the density and thickness of the PTFE NF mats can be effectively tuned by adjusting the stretching process.

citation.title: Stretched PTFE nanofiber nonwovens for superior air permeability and dust holding capacity in PM filtration

content: pectively. These findings demonstrate that the density and thickness of the PTFE NF mats can be effectively tuned by adjusting the stretching process. It is note­worthy that the reduction of thickness and fiber diameter generally contributes to lowering the pressure drop, which is a major concern for nanofibrous nonwoven media used in filtration applications [18,24]. 3.3. Pore structure and mechanical properties of PTFE and sPTFE NFs To investigate the effect of stretching ratio on internal pore archi­tecture of PTFE and sPTFE NFs, the pore diameter distribution and porosity were measured using capillary flow porometry and mercury porosimetry. As shown in Fig. 3A, the pore diameter distribution shifts rightward with increasing stretching ratio, indicating an overall increase of the pore sizes. Specifically, the unstretched PTFE NF exhibited a mean pore diameter of 1.81 μm, while the 2-sPTFE and 3-sPTFE NFs showed increased mean pore diameters of 2.12 and 2.87 μm, respectively. Likewise, the porosity of 3-sPTFE NF was 75.4%, which was clearly higher than that of PTFE and 2-sPTFE NFs at 52.9% and 70.6%, respectively (Figs. 3B and S6). These expanded internal pore properties suggest that the sPTFE NFs based filter media can effectively support depth filtration of particulate contaminants while allowing smooth airflow, potentially resulting in a higher dust holding capacity.

citation.title: Stretched PTFE nanofiber nonwovens for superior air permeability and dust holding capacity in PM filtration

content: fectively support depth filtration of particulate contaminants while allowing smooth airflow, potentially resulting in a higher dust holding capacity. To evaluate the impact of the stretching process on the mechanical properties of the NFs, a tensile test was conducted. As shown in Fig. 3C, the unstretched PTFE NF exhibited the highest tensile stress (10.03 MPa) with a break strain of 268.13%. In comparison, 2-sPTFE and 3-sPTFE NFs showed reduced mechanical properties, with tensile strengths of 6.18 and 2.34 MPa, and break strains of 176.36% and 70.27%, respec­tively. The progressive decrease in both tensile stress and strain decreased from PTFE NF to 2-sPTFE NF and 3-sPTFE NF can be attrib­uted to the thinning of both individual fiber diameter and overall nonwoven thickness. Nevertheless, the retention of the overall nano­fibrous network highlights the superior ductility and high processability of PTFE NFs, as evidenced by their ability to withstand 3-fold stretching without structural failure. These results demonstrate that the controlled stretching process enables the fine-tuning of PTFE NF mechanical properties, making them adaptable for various application requiring a balance between flexibility, durability, and high filtration efficiency. To investigate the effects of thermomechanical stretching on the crystalline properties and polymeric structure of nanofibrous PTFE, differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) and X-ray diffraction (XRD) analyses were further performed.

citation.title: Stretched PTFE nanofiber nonwovens for superior air permeability and dust holding capacity in PM filtration

content: ties and polymeric structure of nanofibrous PTFE, differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) and X-ray diffraction (XRD) analyses were further performed. As shown in Figs. 3D and S7, DSC heating curves of PTFE and sPTFE NFs were obtained at a heating rate of 10 ◦C min-1 over the temperature range of 30–400 ◦C. The melting temperatures (Tm) of the samples were determined to be 329.99, 330.37, and 330.38 ◦C for PTFE, 2-sPTFE, and 3-sPTFE NFs, respectively. The melting enthalpy (ΔHm) values were measured as 38.30, 42.06, and 49.55 J g-1 for PTFE, 2-sPTFE, and 3-sPTFE NFs, respectively. These increases in both Tm and ΔHm with higher stretching ratios indicate an enhancement in the crystallinity of PTFE polymeric chains. The degree of crystallinity was determined by normalizing the measured ΔHm values against a reference value of 80 J g-1 (corresponding to 100%

citation.title: Stretched PTFE nanofiber nonwovens for superior air permeability and dust holding capacity in PM filtration

content: Fig. 3. (A) Pore diameter distribution and mean pore diameter, (B) porosity values, (C) tensile stress-strain curves, (D) DSC heating curves, (E) XRD patterns, and (F) crystallinity determined by DSC and (100) plane crystal size calculated from XRD of PTFE, 2-sPTFE, and 3-sPTFE NFs. J.H. Lee et al. Journal of Membrane Science 733 (2025) 124292 crystallinity of PTFE) [25,26], as detailed in the experimental section. The calculated crystallinity for 3-sPTFE NF was 61.9%, which was higher than that of PTFE and 2-sPTFE NFs (47.9% and 52.6%, respectively). Fig. 3E presents the XRD pattern of PTFE, 2-sPTFE, and 3-sPTFE NFs. All samples exhibited diffraction peaks at consistent 2θ positions, with the (100), (110), and (200) reflections appearing at approximately 18◦, 32◦, and 37◦, respectively, in agreement with the reference pattern (JCPDF card no. 00-045-1594) [27,28]. Notably, the (100) reflection was the most intense and became increasingly sharper with an stretch­ing ratio, whereas the (110) peak, exhibited splitting in PTFE and 2-sPTFE NFs, showed a noticeable reduction in 3-sPTFE NF. This phe­nomenon is attributed to the reorientation of crystallites, which were initially subjected to heterogeneous stress conditions, into a more uni­form alignment during stretching, thereby minimizing internal lattice stress differences [29].

citation.title: Stretched PTFE nanofiber nonwovens for superior air permeability and dust holding capacity in PM filtration

content: ted to heterogeneous stress conditions, into a more uni­form alignment during stretching, thereby minimizing internal lattice stress differences [29]. Furthermore, crystal sizes along the (100) plane were calculated using the equation detailed in the experimental section, yielding values of 36.6, 44.7, and 50.3 nm for PTFE, 2-sPTFE, and 3-sPTFE NFs, respectively (Fig. 3F). These findings demonstrate that the thermomechanical stretching process enhances molecular chain orien­tation and structural stabilization in the semi-crystalline PTFE, as evidenced by the progressive increases in both crystallinity and crystal size. However, in porous nonwoven mats, despite high crystallinity and crystal size, stretching induced thinning and fibrillation decrease the load-bearing solid phase, making it difficult to establish a direct corre­lation with tensile stress. 3.4. Filtration performances of PTFE and sPTFE NFs compared to ePTFE membrane To evaluate the impact of stretching on the filtration performance of PTFE and sPTFE NFs, several filtration tests were performed and compared with a commercial ePTFE membrane (Fig. S8). Initially, the unstretched PTFE NF exhibited the lowest air permeability of 3.1 cm3 cm-2 s-1, due to its dense and thick fibrous network. Notably, the air permeabilities of the 2-sPTFE NF and 3-sPTFE NF significantly increased to 7.1 and 23.3 cm3 cm-2 s-1, respectively (Fig.

citation.title: Stretched PTFE nanofiber nonwovens for superior air permeability and dust holding capacity in PM filtration

content: ous network. Notably, the air permeabilities of the 2-sPTFE NF and 3-sPTFE NF significantly increased to 7.1 and 23.3 cm3 cm-2 s-1, respectively (Fig. 4A), which was considerably higher than that of the commercial ePTFE membrane (5.2 cm3 cm-2 s-1). Given the similar thickness levels of the ePTFE mem­brane and 3-sPTFE NF, the approximately 4.5-fold enhancement in air permeability observed in 3-sPTFE NF is particularly remarkable. The filtration efficiency and pressure drop of the prepared samples were

citation.title: Stretched PTFE nanofiber nonwovens for superior air permeability and dust holding capacity in PM filtration

content: Fig. 4. (A) Air permeability, (B) filtration efficiency/pressure drop, and (C) quality factor (Qf) values of PTFE NF, 2-sPTFE NF, 3-sPTFE NF, and ePTFE membrane. Long-term filtration performance: (D) filtration efficiency, (E and F) pressure drop results, and (G) dust holding capacity of PTFE NF, 2-sPTFE NF, 3-sPTFE NF, and ePTFE membrane after long-term aerosol loading test. (H) Schematic illustration of deposited aerosol on PTFE NF, 2-sPTFE NF, 3-sPTFE NF, and ePTFE membrane as industrial air filters. J.H. Lee et al. Journal of Membrane Science 733 (2025) 124292 assessed using an automated air filtration tester (TSI 8130) at a flow rate of 32 L min-1. As shown in Fig. 4B, the PTFE NF, 2-sPTFE NF, and 3sPTFE NF demonstrated filtration efficiencies of 97.4%, 94.3%, and 92.7%, respectively, indicating that the pore enlargement due to the stretching led to a slight reduction in filtration efficiency. Similarly, the pressure drop, which typically exhibits a trade-off relationship with filtration efficiency, decreased from 161.8 Pa (PTFE NF) to 79.4 Pa (2sPTFE NF) and further to 60.8 Pa (3-sPTFE NF). These findings suggest that key factors such as thickness and fiber density in the filtration media are closely related to filtration performances, as corroborated by SEM images and observed pore properties. The commercial ePTFE membrane exhibited a relatively high filtration efficiency of 98.8%, however, it suffered from a pressure drop of 150.1 Pa.

citation.title: Stretched PTFE nanofiber nonwovens for superior air permeability and dust holding capacity in PM filtration

content: ties. The commercial ePTFE membrane exhibited a relatively high filtration efficiency of 98.8%, however, it suffered from a pressure drop of 150.1 Pa. A comprehensive evaluation of the overall filtration performance was conducted using the quality factor (Qf), calculated based on the equation described in the experimental section. As shown in Fig. 4C, the Qf values of the PTFE NF, 2-sPTFE NF, and 3-sPTFE NF were determined to be 0.023, 0.036, and 0.043 Pa-1, respectively. Despite the ePTFE membrane achieving the highest filtration efficiency, its Qf value was only 0.029 Pa-1, which is slightly higher than that of the unstretched PTFE NF. Notably, both the 2-sPTFE NF and 3-sPTFE NF demonstrated significantly improved Qf values, underscoring their potential advan­tages for practical applications in electrospun and stretched NF-based filtration media. Furthermore, to evaluate the effect of thickness normalization on filtration performance, 6-layered 3-sPTFE NF (176.8 μm) and ePTFE membrane (177.8 μm) were tested under the same filtration conditions. As shown in Fig. S9A, both multilayer samples achieved highly enhanced filtration efficiency of 99.99%, compared to single-layer PTFE NF (97.4%), while exhibiting pressure drops of 280.7 and 767.2 Pa, respectively.

citation.title: Stretched PTFE nanofiber nonwovens for superior air permeability and dust holding capacity in PM filtration

content: hanced filtration efficiency of 99.99%, compared to single-layer PTFE NF (97.4%), while exhibiting pressure drops of 280.7 and 767.2 Pa, respectively. Notably, the 6-layered 3-sPTFE NF main­tained the highest Qf (0.033 Pa-1) despite a 461.7% increase in pressure drop relative to the single-layer, confirming once again that its hierar­chical internal structure effectively mitigates resistance increase (Fig. S9B). Furthermore, long-term filtration performance tests were conducted to evaluate the changes in filtration efficiency, pressure drop, and the dust holding capacity of all samples as a function of NaCl aerosol loading time. The continuous loading test was carried out until the maximum pressure drop reached 1,471 Pa (150 mmH2O). At that point, the pore space of samples were almost stucked by the continuously deposited NaCl aerosol particles (Fig. S10), regarding them lose their airpermeable filtration functions. As indicated in Fig. 4D, three NF based filters exhibited gradual increases in filtration efficiency, reaching 99% within 3,700 s. They maintained high filtration efficiency throughout the loading test until reaching the maximum pressure drop, with oper­ational lifetimes of 13,780, 14,586, and 16,281 s, respectively.

citation.title: Stretched PTFE nanofiber nonwovens for superior air permeability and dust holding capacity in PM filtration

content: iency throughout the loading test until reaching the maximum pressure drop, with oper­ational lifetimes of 13,780, 14,586, and 16,281 s, respectively. In contrast, the ePTFE membrane reached a filtration efficiency of nearly 99% within just 600 s from the start of the loading test, which acceler­ated clogging and shortened its operational lifetime to 5,440 s, approximately one third that of the 3-sPTFE NF filter. Figs. 4E and F, and S11 comparatively evaluate the pressure drop in the samples over the entire, the 600 s from the initial state for the loading test, and the 600 s preceding the maximum pressure drop, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4E and F, the PTFE NF filter exhibited the highest pressure drop of 150.1 Pa within 130 s from the start. Never­theless, the ePTFE membrane surpassed PTFE NF filter of 175.5 Pa at 130 s, with rapid initial (13.8 Pa min-1) and later (10.1 Pa min-1) slopes. In contrast, our NF filters exhibited relatively moderate initial slopes (7.2, 4.6, and 3.3 Pa min-1 for PTFE NF, 2-sPTFE NF, and 3-sPTFE NF filters, respectively), followed by more gradual slopes (1.7, 1.7, and 1.5 Pa min-1 for PTFE NF, 2-sPTFE NF, and 3-sPTFE NF filters, respec­tively) before the end, contributing to an extended lifetime (Fig. S10).

citation.title: Stretched PTFE nanofiber nonwovens for superior air permeability and dust holding capacity in PM filtration

content: 1.7, and 1.5 Pa min-1 for PTFE NF, 2-sPTFE NF, and 3-sPTFE NF filters, respec­tively) before the end, contributing to an extended lifetime (Fig. S10). These findings suggest that NF based filters with micron-scale pores further expand the internal porous space through the stretching process, creating a structure that facilitates particle capture across the entire thickness of the media, which is characteristic of depth filtration. In contrast, the ePTFE membrane, due to its extremely high fibrous density, primarily captures particles at the surface, a characteristic of surface filtration, ultimately leading to rapid clogging and a significantly reduced operational lifetime. Considering the differences in structure and pore properties, the dust holding capacity was further evaluated after the loading test, as described in the experimental section. In general, dust holding capacity is a crucial indicator of the service life of filtration materials and is widely used to assess the application performance of fresh air filters [30,31]. As shown in Fig. 4G, the dust holding capacity of the ePTFE membrane was only 29.8 g m-2, while the PTFE NF achieved a signifi­cantly higher value of 84.2 g m-2.

citation.title: Stretched PTFE nanofiber nonwovens for superior air permeability and dust holding capacity in PM filtration

content: Fig. 4G, the dust holding capacity of the ePTFE membrane was only 29.8 g m-2, while the PTFE NF achieved a signifi­cantly higher value of 84.2 g m-2. This enhancement can be attributed to the filtration mechanism; the ePTFE membrane primarily intercept aerosols on its surface (surface-mediated filtration), whereas the fibrous nonwoven filters such as NFs utilize their internal pore spaces within their bulky structure to capture a substantial amount of particles, following a depth filtration mechanism, as illustrated in Fig. 4H. Notably, the dust holding capacity of the sPTFE NFs further increased with the stretching process, reaching 89.3 and 107.7 g m-2 for 2-sPTFE and 3-sPTFE NFs, respectively. This improvement in dust holding ca­pacity suggests an extended service life, making sPTFE NFs highly promising candidates for industrial air filtration. 3.5. Aerosol deposition simulation using SEM image-based 3D modeling of PTFE NF and 3-sPTFE NF filters A simulation analysis of aerosol deposition was conducted to visu­alize and understand the mechanism contributing to the highperformance air filtration of sPTFE NF-based filters. As illustrated in Figs. S12 and 2D modeling along the Xand Z-directions was performed for each sample (PTFE NF and 3-sPTFE NF) using cross-sectional and top-view SEM images. Key parameters, including thickness, fibrer diameter, pore size distribution, and porosity, were incorporated into the model.

citation.title: Stretched PTFE nanofiber nonwovens for superior air permeability and dust holding capacity in PM filtration

content: and top-view SEM images. Key parameters, including thickness, fibrer diameter, pore size distribution, and porosity, were incorporated into the model. Subsequently, 3D models for PTFE and 3-sPTFE NFs were constructed by configuring the voxel count (523 * 363 * 2200 for PTFE NF and 523 * 363 * 744 for 3-sPTFE NF), voxel length (80 nm for both), and solidity values calculated from porosity measurements. As shown in Fig. 5A and B, the generated 3D models were precisely configured with an area of 100 cm2, faithfully replicating the actual experimental results as summarized in Table S2. To perform the aerosol deposition simula­tion, aerosols with an average particle size of 260 nm, identical to that of NaCl particles used, were initially distributed over the top surface, ensuring a consistent deposition environment at a flow rate of 32 L min-1. Based on these boundary conditions, the dynamic aerosol deposition behavior was continuously monitored in real time (Supporting Videos 1and 2), and both filtration efficiency and pressure drop were simulated. As shown in Fig. 5C, the PTFE NF exhibited aerosol capture throughout its entire thickness, however, the deposition was less uniform due to its densely packed fiber distribution. In contrast, despite its reduced overall thickness, the 3-sPTFE NF demonstrated uniformly distributed aerosol capture, resulting in a more greater amount of aerosol deposition within confined regions owing to its expanded pore structure (Fig. 5D).

citation.title: Stretched PTFE nanofiber nonwovens for superior air permeability and dust holding capacity in PM filtration

content: uted aerosol capture, resulting in a more greater amount of aerosol deposition within confined regions owing to its expanded pore structure (Fig. 5D). This trend aligns with the dust holding capacity values presented in Fig. 4G. The simulated filtration efficiencies of PTFE and 3-sPTFE NFs were 97.6% and 92.8%, respectively, which closely matched the experimental values. However, the simulated pressure drops for PTFE and 3-sPTFE NFs were 922.9 and 185.7 Pa, respectively, which were much higher than the experimentally measured values. This gap arises from limita­tions in the modeling method, which is based on stacking 2D SEM im­ages that only produce a discontinuous fibrous structure, lacking the true connectivity of an actual nonwoven network [32–35]. Nevertheless, the overall trend of changes in filtration efficiency and pressure drop following stretching was meaningfully obtained. As presented in Fig. 5E and F, the reduction rate in filtration efficiency from PTFE NF to 3-sPTFE NF was 4.7%, closely matching the experimental reduction rate of 4.8%,

citation.title: Stretched PTFE nanofiber nonwovens for superior air permeability and dust holding capacity in PM filtration

content: J.H. Lee et al. Journal of Membrane Science 733 (2025) 124292 while the pressure drop showed a reduction rate of 79.9%, compared to the experimental value of 62.4%. Despite some variations, both samples exhibited the same overall trend, validating the reliability of the aerosol deposition simulation. These results confirm that both PTFE and 3-sPTFE NFs operate via depth filtration mechanisms. It is noted that the densely packed fine pores of PTFE NF can lead to fiber congestion and unfavorable dead zones for aerosol deposition, potentially causing a substantial increase in pressure drop. In contrast, the expanded pore structure of 3-sPTFE NF appears to mitigate these limitations, suggesting that it can significantly reduce pressure drop while maintaining excel­lent filtration efficiency, thereby facilitating the practical application of nanofibrous air filters. 4. Conclusion In summary, we successfully developed highly efficient stretched PTFE NFs using a novel hybrid stretching technique following emulsi­fication electrospinning. The stretching process induced substantial microstructural transformations in the PTFE NFs, resulting in a reduc­tion in overall filter thickness and fiber diameter, as well as an increase in internal pore size. These modifications enhanced air permeability while preserving and reinforcing the inherent crystalline stability of nanofibrous PTFE.

citation.title: Stretched PTFE nanofiber nonwovens for superior air permeability and dust holding capacity in PM filtration

content: rnal pore size. These modifications enhanced air permeability while preserving and reinforcing the inherent crystalline stability of nanofibrous PTFE. As a result, the 3-fold stretched PTFE (3-sPTFE) NF achieved a filtration efficiency exceeding 92%, a significantly reduced pressure drop of 60.8 Pa, an extended operational lifetime of 16,821 s, and a dust holding capacity of 107.7 g m-2, significantly outperforming conventional ePTFE membrane, which rely primarily on surface filtra­tion. Moreover, 3D modeling-based simulations confirmed that the optimized microstructure and uniform pore distribution ensure consis­tent and efficient filtration performance. We believe that our findings provide a valuable framework for the rational design of PTFE NFs with tailored properties, enhancing their performance in advanced filtration systems. Moreover, the exceptional thermal and chemical stability of sPTFE NFs expands their potential for use in diverse membrane applications, including high temperature, chemically aggressive, and energy efficient separation processes.
